claim_text: The proportion of cells expressing Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice in both FACS and droplet datasets.
evidence_location: Figure 2a-d; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590-591)
supporting_quote: The proportion of cells expressing Cdkn2a more than doubled in older mice compared with younger mice according to analysis by both FACS (Fig. 2a) and droplet (Fig. 2b) methods; this was accompanied by a twofold increase in the expression levels of p16 in cells in which it was expressed (Fig. 2c, d).
key_terms: Cdkn2a: gene encoding the p16 tumor suppressor protein; a widely used marker of cellular senescence

claim_text: The mean expression level of Cdkn2a approximately doubled in cells that express it when comparing old to young mice in both FACS and droplet data.
evidence_location: Figure 2c-d; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590-591)
supporting_quote: this was accompanied by a twofold increase in the expression levels of p16 in cells in which it was expressed (Fig. 2c, d).

claim_text: In 30-month-old mice the fraction of cells expressing Cdkn2a was smaller than in 24-month-old mice.
evidence_location: Figure 2b; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590-591)
supporting_quote: Notably, in 30-month-old mice the fraction of cells that expressed p16 was smaller than in 24-month-old mice, perhaps because long-living animals have a slower rate of senescence.

claim_text: Among previously characterized senescence markers, Cdkn2a showed the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed.
evidence_location: Main text - paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
supporting_quote: Cdkn2a has the highest correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells in which it is expressed; other genes with positive correlation include E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf and Itgax.

claim_text: The fraction of cells expressing members of the Sirt family (Sirt3, Sirt4, Sirt5) decreased with age.
evidence_location: Main text - paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
supporting_quote: For some genes, including members of the Sirt family (Sirt3, Sirt4 and Sirt5), the fraction of cells in which they were expressed was found to decrease with age

claim_text: In the bladder, the mesenchymal cell compartment decreased by approximately threefold over the mouse lifespan while the urothelial compartment increased by a similar amount.
evidence_location: Figure 2e; Main text - bladder composition (page 591)
supporting_quote: whereas the mesenchymal compartment of this tissue decreases by a factor of three over the lifetime of the mouse (Fig. 2e, left), the urothelial compartment increases by a similar amount (Fig. 2e, right).

claim_text: Differential gene expression analysis of the bladder showed downregulation of stromal-associated genes (Col1a1, Col1a2, Col3a1, Dcn) and upregulation of epithelial-associated genes (Krt15, Krt18, Sfn) with age.
evidence_location: Figure 2f; Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: Using differential gene expression analysis to assess overall changes in tissues with age, we found that stromal-associated genes (Col1a1, Col1a2, Col3a1 and Dcn) are downregulated while epithelial-associated genes (Krt15, Krt18 and Sfn) are upregulated, supporting the compositional observations

claim_text: The bladder endothelial cell population declined with age, and vasculature-associated genes Htra1 and Fos were downregulated.
evidence_location: Figure 2e-f; Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: The decline of the endothelial population suggests that bladder ageing in mice might be associated with lower organ vascularization, which is consistent with recent findings and with the observed downregulation of vasculature-associated genes Htra1 and Fos

claim_text: The bladder leukocyte population increased with age, accompanied by overexpression of Lgals3, Igfbp2, and Ly6d across the tissue.
evidence_location: Figure 2e-f; Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: The increase in the leukocyte population could indicate an inflammatory tissue microenvironment, a common hallmark of ageing that is consistent with literature on overactive bladders and is supported by a significant overexpression of Lgals3, Igfbp2 and Ly6d across the tissue

claim_text: Old bladder leukocytes showed increased expression of pro-inflammatory markers (Cd14, Lgals3, Tnfrsf12a) and decreased expression of anti-inflammatory markers (Cd9, Cd81) compared with young leukocytes.
evidence_location: Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: we observed that old leukocytes show increased expression of pro-inflammatory markers-such as Cd14, Lgals3 and Tnfrsf12a-and decreased expression of anti-inflammatory markers such as Cd9 and Cd81

claim_text: In the kidney, the relative abundances of mesangial cells, capillary endothelial cells, loop of Henle ascending limb epithelial cells, and loop of Henle thick ascending limb epithelial cells all decreased with age.
evidence_location: Figure 2g; Main text - kidney composition (page 591-592); Supplementary Table 5
supporting_quote: Age-related changes in the kidney include a decrease in the relative abundance of mesangial cells, capillary endothelial cells, loop of Henle ascending limb epithelial cells and loop of Henle thick ascending limb epithelial cells

claim_text: Tissue-wide expression of Egf and Atp1a1 decreased with age in the kidney.
evidence_location: Figure 2h; Main text - kidney composition (page 591-592); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: together with a tissue-wide reduction of Egf and Atp1a1 expression (Fig. 2h, Supplementary Table 6)-suggest an impaired glomerular filtration rate

claim_text: Local Atp1a1 expression increased with age in kidney capillary endothelial cells and mesangial cells despite the declining proportion of these cell types.
evidence_location: Main text - kidney composition (page 591-592); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: Notably, local Atp1a1 expression increases with age in both capillary endothelial cells and mesangial cells, suggesting that a compensation mechanism counteracts the effects of the declining proportion of these cells with age.

claim_text: Tissue-wide expression of Umod decreased with age in the kidney, while Umod expression increased within the thick ascending limb epithelial cells that became less abundant.
evidence_location: Figure 2h; Main text - kidney composition (page 592); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: the expression of Umod-which encodes uromodulin, the most abundant protein in urine-is also reduced across the tissue. The protein uromodulin is produced by the epithelial cells that line the thick ascending limb, and therefore-given the relative decrease in the proportion of epithelial cells in the ascending and the thick ascending limb-our results suggest that normal kidney functions are impaired

claim_text: In the spleen, the proportion of T cells decreased with age while the relative amount of plasma cells increased.
evidence_location: Figure 2i; Main text - spleen composition (page 592); Supplementary Table 5
supporting_quote: In the spleen, the proportion of T cells decreases with age while the relative amount of plasma cells increases (Fig. 2i).

claim_text: Spleen differential gene expression showed upregulation of B cell and plasma cell markers (Cd79a, Jchain) and downregulation of the T cell marker Cd3d with age.
evidence_location: Figure 2j; Main text - spleen composition (page 592); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: This is supported by the upregulation of B cell and plasma cell marker genes (Cd79a and Jchain (also known as Igj), respectively; Fig. 2j, Supplementary Table 6) and the downregulation of Cd3d
key_terms: Jchain: gene encoding the joining chain of immunoglobulins (also known as Igj)

claim_text: The T cell population in the mammary gland declined with age.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5c; Main text - mammary gland (page 592-593)
supporting_quote: Similarly, in the mammary gland we observed a decline of the T cell population (Extended Data Fig. 5c).

claim_text: AP1 transcription factor genes (Junb, Jund, Fos) were upregulated with age in the mammary gland.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5d; Main text - mammary gland (page 592-593); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: Moreover, genes encoding AP1 transcription factors (Junb, Jund and Fos) were upregulated with age (Extended Data Fig. 5d, Supplementary Table 6)
key_terms: AP1: activator protein 1 transcription factor complex

claim_text: The relative proportion of hepatocytes in the liver decreased with age.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6a; Main text - liver composition (page 593)
supporting_quote: We observed an age-related decrease in the relative number of hepatocytes (Extended Data Fig. 6a-d), which is supported by the reduction in the expression of albumin (Alb; Extended Data Fig. 6e, Supplementary Table 6).

claim_text: Liver differential gene expression revealed an increased immune signature with age, including overexpression of H2-Aa, H2-Ab1, H2-D1, H2-Eb1, Cd74, and Lyz2.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6e; Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: Differential gene expression analysis revealed an increased immune signature, as illustrated by the overexpression of H2-Aa, H2-Ab1, H2-D1, H2-Eb1, Cd74, Lyz2 and others

claim_text: The number of Clec4f-positive Kupffer cells in the liver did not change with age, but the number of cells co-expressing Clec4f and Il1b increased with age.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6g-j; Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 7
supporting_quote: We performed in situ RNA staining in liver Kupffer cells (Extended Data Fig. 6g) with Clec4f and found that the number of Clec4f-positive cells does not change with age, consistent with the results of the tissue composition analysis (Extended Data Fig. 6h, Supplementary Table 7). However, when co-staining with Il1b, we found that the number of cells expressing both Clec4f and Il1b increased with age

claim_text: Mrc1 expression increased with age in liver Kupffer cells, while overall Mrc1 expression in liver endothelial cells decreased with age.
evidence_location: Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: We identified increased expression of Mrc1 with age in Kupffer cells, whereas the overall expression of Mrc1 in liver endothelial cells decreased with age

claim_text: The number of Mrc1-expressing liver sinusoidal endothelial cells increased with age as shown by in situ RNA staining.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6l-o; Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 7
supporting_quote: we found that the number of Mrc1-expressing LSECs increased with age (Extended Data Fig. 6m-o).

claim_text: An age-related increase in the number of somatic mutations was observed across all analysed organs using full-length transcript data from FACS.
evidence_location: Figure 3; Extended Data Figure 7; Main text - genomic instability / mutations (page 593)
supporting_quote: We focused on genes that were expressed in at least 75% of cells for each age group within a particular tissue, and observed an age-related increase in the number of mutations across all of the organs we analysed (Fig. 3, Extended Data Fig. 7a, c, e)

claim_text: Tongue and bladder were the most affected organs in terms of age-related accumulation of somatic mutations.
evidence_location: Main text - genomic instability / mutations (page 593); Extended Data Figure 7
supporting_quote: tongue and bladder were the most affected.

claim_text: The observed age-related increase in mutations exceeded the level expected from amplification and sequencing errors as estimated using ERCC spike-in controls.
evidence_location: Figure 3; Extended Data Figures 7-8; Main text - genomic instability / mutations (page 593)
supporting_quote: we verified that the number of mutations exceeded those expected from amplification and sequencing errors, which can be estimated using External RNA Controls Consortium (ERCC) controls that were spiked into each well
key_terms: ERCC: External RNA Controls Consortium spike-in RNA standards used to estimate technical error rates

claim_text: B cell receptor sequences were computationally assembled for 6050 cells from FACS data across three time points.
evidence_location: Figure 4a; Main text - immune repertoire (page 593-594)
supporting_quote: B cell receptors were assembled for 6,050 cells (Fig. 4a, Extended Data Fig. 9a)

claim_text: The fraction of B cells belonging to a clonal family increased from approximately 9% at 3 months to approximately 20% at 18 months and approximately 38% at 24 months.
evidence_location: Figure 4a; Main text - immune repertoire (page 593-594); Supplementary Table 9
supporting_quote: whereas most of the cells at 3 months were not part of a clone (9% were part of a clonal family), the number of B cells belonging to a clonotype doubled at 18 months (20%) when compared to 3 months, and doubled again from 18 months to 24 months (around 38%).

claim_text: T cell receptor sequences were computationally assembled for 6000 cells from FACS data across three time points.
evidence_location: Figure 4b; Main text - immune repertoire (page 593-594)
supporting_quote: T cell receptors for 6,000 cells (Fig. 4b, Extended Data Fig. 9b).

claim_text: The fraction of T cells belonging to a clonal family increased from approximately 3% at 3 months to approximately 23% at 18 months and approximately 20% at 24 months.
evidence_location: Figure 4b; Main text - immune repertoire (page 593-594); Supplementary Table 9
supporting_quote: only around 3% (55 out of 1,895) of the cells at 3 months were part of a clone. At 18 months and 24 months, this value increased to around 23% (479 out of 2,056) and around 20% (348 out of 1,780) of the cells, respectively

claim_text: Transcriptomic diversity analysis identified brain myeloid microglial cells and kidney macrophages as cell types with significant age-related changes in diversity.
evidence_location: Figure 4c; Extended Data Figure 10; Extended Data Figure 11a-b; Main text - diversity score (page 593-594)
supporting_quote: We observed significant changes in diversity for cells of the immune system that originate from the brain and the kidney (Fig. 4c, Extended Data Fig. 11a, b).

claim_text: The age-related diversity changes in brain microglia and kidney macrophages were not confounded by the number of genes expressed per cell.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 11c-d; Main text - diversity score (page 594)
supporting_quote: These results were not confounded by the number of genes expressed per cell (Extended Data Fig. 11c, d).

claim_text: In brain myeloid microglial cells, young (3-month) microglia predominantly occupied distinct transcriptomic clusters from old (18-month and 24-month) microglia.
evidence_location: Figure 4d; Main text - brain microglia (page 594)
supporting_quote: In brain myeloid microglial cells, the majority of young (3 month) microglia occupy clusters 1 and 6, whereas old (18 month and 24 month) microglia constitute the vast majority of cells in clusters 10, 12 and 14

claim_text: Trajectory analysis suggested that young microglia transition through an intermediate state before acquiring the transcriptomic signature of old microglia.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 11e; Main text - brain microglia (page 594)
supporting_quote: Trajectory analysis suggests that young microglia go through an intermediate state, represented by the clusters mostly occupied by 18-month microglial cells, before acquiring the signature of old microglia

claim_text: Old microglial clusters upregulated MHC class I genes (H2-D1, H2-K1, B2m) and genes associated with degenerative disease such as Fth1.
evidence_location: Main text - brain microglia (page 594); Supplementary Table 10
supporting_quote: These cells upregulate major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I genes (H2-D1, H2-K1 and B2m), along with genes associated with degenerative disease (for example, Fth1)

claim_text: Old microglial clusters were enriched for interferon-responsive or regulatory genes including Oasl2, Oas1a, Ifit3, Rtp4, Bst2, Stat1, Irf7, Ifitm3, Usp18, Ifi204, and Ifit2.
evidence_location: Main text - brain microglia (page 594); Supplementary Table 10
supporting_quote: gene expression in clusters 10, 12 and 14 is enriched with interferon responsive or regulatory genes (for example, Oasl2, Oas1a, Ifit3, Rtp4, Bst2, Stat1, Irf7, Ifitm3, Usp18, Ifi204 and Ifit2), suggesting an expansion of this small pro-inflammatory subset of microglia in the ageing brain

claim_text: Approximately 55 of the top 200 differentially expressed genes between young and old microglial clusters overlapped with a previously reported Alzheimer's disease-specific microglial signature.
evidence_location: Figure 4e; Main text - brain microglia (page 594); Supplementary Table 10
supporting_quote: the list of differentially expressed genes between 'young' and 'old' clusters resembles the previously reported Alzheimer's disease-specific microglial signature, with 55 out of the top 200 differential expressed genes shared between the two differential gene expression lists

claim_text: In kidney macrophages, a cluster predominantly composed of young (1-month and 3-month) cells was enriched for an M2-macrophage gene signature, while a cluster predominantly composed of old (18-month and older) cells resembled an M1-proinflammatory macrophage state.
evidence_location: Figure 4f; Extended Data Figure 11f; Main text - kidney macrophages (page 594); Supplementary Table 10
supporting_quote: Differential gene expression revealed that cluster 10 is enriched for an M2-macrophage gene signature (for example, Il10, H2-Eb1, H2-Ab1, H2-Aa, Cd74, C1qa, Cxcl16, Hexb, Cd81, C1qb and Cd72) whereas cluster 13 resembles a M1-proinflammatory macrophage state (for example, Hp, Itgal, Msrb1 and Gngt2)
key_terms: M2-macrophage: anti-inflammatory macrophage phenotype; M1-macrophage: pro-inflammatory macrophage phenotype

claim_text: Changes in gene expression observed in bulk experiments are often attributable to both changes in the number of cells of a given type and changes in per-cell gene expression levels.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5a-b; Main text - paragraph 3 (page 590)
supporting_quote: we observed that-in many cases-changes in gene expression are due to both changes in the numbers of cells in a population and changes in the gene expression levels in each cell

claim_text: B cells and endothelial cells independently annotated in different organs clustered together by unbiased whole-transcriptome analysis irrespective of tissue of origin or processing method.
evidence_location: Figure 1e-f; Extended Data Figure 1g-l
supporting_quote: cells annotated as B cells or endothelial cells tend to occupy the same clusters irrespective of their tissue of origin or the method with which they were processed

claim_text: More than 70% of cells in the dataset were automatically annotated using the Tabula Muris 3-month reference, with the remainder annotated manually by tissue experts.
evidence_location: Figure 1b; Main text - paragraph 2 (page 590)
supporting_quote: Using this approach, we were able to automatically annotate more than 70% of the cells. All the automated cell annotations were reviewed and approved by human experts, and the remaining cells were annotated by hand

claim_text: The relative abundance of precursor B cells in bone marrow decreased significantly with age.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5e; Supplementary Table 5
supporting_quote: Relative abundances of marrow precursor B cells change significantly with age

claim_text: The relative abundance of keratinocyte stem cells in skin changed significantly with age.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 5g; Supplementary Table 5
supporting_quote: Relative abundances of skin keratinocyte stem cells change significantly with age

claim_text: Hepatocyte cell size increased and cell number decreased with age in the liver as shown by bright-field imaging.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6b-d
supporting_quote: Bright-field imaging of hepatocytes at different ages (b) and respective quantification (c, d).

claim_text: Albumin (Alb) expression in the liver decreased with age, consistent with the declining proportion of hepatocytes.
evidence_location: Extended Data Figure 6e; Main text - liver composition (page 593); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: We observed an age-related decrease in the relative number of hepatocytes (Extended Data Fig. 6a-d), which is supported by the reduction in the expression of albumin (Alb; Extended Data Fig. 6e, Supplementary Table 6).

claim_text: Immune response genes Tnfrsf12a and Cdkn1a were overexpressed with age in both bladder mesenchymal cells and bladder urothelial cells.
evidence_location: Main text - bladder composition (page 591); Supplementary Table 6
supporting_quote: the overexpression of genes associated with immune response-such as Tnfrsf12a and Cdkn1a-in both bladder (mesenchymal) cells and bladder urothelial cells

claim_text: The age-related increase in somatic mutations was observed across all five functional cell-type groups (endothelial, immune, parenchymal, stem/progenitor, and stromal cells).
evidence_location: Figure 3b-f; Extended Data Figure 7
supporting_quote: Distribution of the difference of the mean mutation in the gene set (and ERCC spike-in controls) per cell between 24 months and 3 months and between 18 months and 3 months for all tissues and cells (a) and with the cell types split into five functional groups: endothelial cells (b), immune cells (c), parenchymal cells (d), stem/progenitor cells (e) and stromal cells (f).

claim_text: Senescence-associated genes E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf, and Itgax showed positive correlation between ageing and the fraction of cells expressing them.
evidence_location: Main text - paragraph 3 (page 591); Supplementary Table 4
supporting_quote: other genes with positive correlation include E2f2, Lmnb1, Tnf and Itgax.
